era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV. Continued reign of Khufu or transition toward his successor (Khafre, r. c. 2558–2532 BCE in conventional dating). Mesopotamia: the Akkad Empire under Sargon of Akkad was consolidating Sumerian city-states. Indus Valley: urban centres at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro were flourishing. This period represents the apex of Old Kingdom centralised power and monumental ambition.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indus Valley — no written records; only archaeological evidence
Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, Americas — no surviving documentation

events.title: Continuation of Giza pyramid complex construction and administration
events.description: Ongoing construction, labour organisation, and administrative oversight of the Giza pyramid complex. Archaeological evidence (quarry marks, worker graffiti, administrative records) indicates sustained, large-scale mobilisation of resources. The pyramids served as focal points for state ideology, religious practice, and demonstration of pharaonic authority.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza (quarry marks, worker villages, administrative records)
Pyramid Texts (religious inscriptions in later pyramids, but reflecting Old Kingdom ideology)

events.certainty_note: The year 2548 BCE falls within the conventional dating of Dynasty IV (c. 2613–2494 BCE), but no specific events for this year are attested. The entry represents a best-estimate placement within a multi-decade construction project.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events for 2548 BCE are documented in surviving sources. The assignment of this year to the Giza construction phase is inferential, based on broader chronological frameworks that themselves are debated. Some scholars place the transition from Khufu to Khafre earlier or later than conventional dating suggests.
historiographic_note: The historical record for Egypt in Dynasty IV is almost entirely monumental and administrative in nature. Year-by-year events are not recoverable from surviving sources. Absolute chronology depends on radiocarbon dating, king lists, and astronomical calculations, all of which carry significant uncertainty (±50–100 years). The surviving record is heavily biased toward elite and royal perspectives.
